role_summary: Joint oral history of six surviving veterans of the 1951 Adkin High School walkout in Kinston, North Carolina, the student-organized mass walkout protesting unequal conditions at the segregated all-Black high school: John Dudley, Eleanor Stewart, Charles Jarmon, Frances Suggs, Harold Suggs, and Samuel Dove. The 1951 walkout was a student-led action planned and staged without adult guidance, three years before Brown v. Board of Education and nine years before the February 1960 Greensboro sit-ins.
ai_reading: The John Dudley et al cohort's top semantic neighbor is Robert L. Carter (entry 108) at a cosine count of 136; the broader cluster includes James Oscar Jones (entry 57), Gertrude Newsome Jackson (entry 136), and Clarence Magee (entry 21). The Robert L. Carter cosine is the catalog's clearest student-walkout-and-NAACP-legal-strategy pairing: Carter argued for Oliver Brown in Brown v. Board of Education three years after the Adkin walkout; the embedding catches the pre-Brown student-direct-action register that the litigation strategy responded to. The novel observation this joint page surfaces is the pre-Brown / pre-sit-in student-walkout signature: the 1951 Adkin walkout predates the more-photographed 1960 Greensboro sit-ins by nine years and Brown v. Board by three; the historiography has often subordinated it to the post-Brown direct-action wave, but the catalog's embedding aligns the 1951 walkout register with the broader student-organized direct-action tradition rather than as a pre-movement anomaly.
biographical_paragraph: The John Dudley et al Civil Rights History Project oral history is cross-referenced against the Library of Congress published transcript. The external biographical record is documented through the Civil Rights History Project finding aid for entry 64 [src: 1, src: 2]. The interviewees are six surviving veterans of the 1951 Adkin High School walkout in Kinston, North Carolina: John Dudley, Eleanor Stewart, Charles Jarmon, Frances Suggs, Harold Suggs, and Samuel Dove. Adkin High School was Kinston's all-Black, segregated high school; the students realized that their school was systematically unequal to the all-white Kinston school they were barred from, and they planned and staged a school-wide walkout and protest march without the assistance of any adults [src: 2]. The walkout was a student-organized direct action three years before Brown v. Board of Education and nine years before the February 1960 Greensboro sit-ins. John Dudley later worked as a youth home director for the Bureau of Rehabilitation in Washington, D.C. The joint interview was conducted by Emilye Crosby in Hyattsville, Maryland in June 2013 [src: 2].

interview_snippets.speaker: John Dudley
interview_snippets.lead_in: John Dudley traces the walkout to a Wednesday civics class at Adkin High School, where the teacher B.C. Davis had the seniors read a weekly-reader article on what an ideal school contains:
interview_snippets.quote: And that day we were doing our weekly reader, and the topic for that day was what an ideal school has. What an ideal school had. So we saw all those items a good school has. We asked Mrs. Davis, is there a school in our area that has these things. She says, yes, the school up on the hill. The white school. Grainger High School.

interview_snippets.speaker: John Dudley
interview_snippets.lead_in: Dudley describes how the senior committee organized the entire student body in secret, telling the other students to keep the plan from parents and teachers:
interview_snippets.quote: We are strategizing and organizing. We said, now if the board didn't give us the proper response, we are going to walk out. We hadn't decided what the proper response would be. So, that week, leading to Monday, we strategized. And we had everybody on board, 720 students. We told them not to tell your parents or your teacher what's going on. And do you believe to this day, 2013, nobody has ever told me that an adult knew what was going on. Kids.

interview_snippets.speaker: John Dudley
interview_snippets.lead_in: Dudley, who read the morning announcements at Adkin, recounts the moment the walkout was triggered the day after the school board rejected the students' petition:
interview_snippets.quote: when I made the announcement, 720 students got out of their seats, orderly, went to pick up their placards that we had already made up in case the board didn't respond properly, and we marching down Queen St., marching down Queen St. No incidents. People curious to what was going on. Teachers running around, "What's going on? What's going on?" And we gone.

interview_snippets.speaker: Samuel Dove
interview_snippets.lead_in: Samuel Dove, the youngest participant, describes a sit-in scheme he staged at Standard Drug number two in Kinston two weeks after the February 1960 Greensboro sit-ins, posing a friend as an African diplomat to get served at the segregated counter:
interview_snippets.quote: I was a senior in high school and I had cooked up kind of a scheme to integrate Standard Drug number two with a couple of friends, brothers. Curtis Henderson and Tom Henderson. I asked Curtis to dress as an African diplomat, and he did. His brother was going to be a lookout. We were going to Standard Drug number two to get him served. I was going to be his interpreter. I knew that Ghana had just gotten its freedom the month before.

interview_snippets.speaker: Eleanor Stewart
interview_snippets.lead_in: Eleanor Stewart, who worked as a waitress at a Kinston drug store where Black customers could not sit down, recalls a small protest she and other students staged at a Rose's five-and-dime that repeatedly served white customers ahead of them:
interview_snippets.quote: So one day, a group of us, we were in there trying to get candy, and they wouldn't wait on us, kept putting us back in the end of then line. So somebody finally said, "Give me this, give me this." All kind of different candy. And then when they packed it up, they said, "We don't want any of it," and just walked out.

movement_context: The 1951 Adkin High walkout sits inside a period of legal and pedagogical pressure on Southern segregated schooling that scholars treat as the institutional run-up to Brown v. Board of Education (1954). Charles Hamilton Houston's NAACP Legal Defense and Educational Fund strategy through the 1940s had used the equal-facilities clause of Plessy v. Ferguson (1896) as a wedge: a sequence of cases (Missouri ex rel. Gaines v. Canada in 1938, Sipuel v. Board of Regents in 1948, Sweatt v. Painter and McLaurin v. Oklahoma State Regents in 1950) forced graduate and professional school admissions of Black students by demonstrating that the separate facility provided to them was materially unequal [src: 3, src: 4]. By 1950, NAACP General Counsel Robert L. Carter (catalog entry 108) and Thurgood Marshall had pivoted from the equalization theory to a frontal challenge to the separate-but-equal doctrine, the litigation strategy that produced the five consolidated K-12 cases adjudicated as Brown v. Board of Education and its companion cases in 1952-1954 [src: 4]. The Adkin students' walkout, in the spring of 1951, occurred within months of the April 1951 student walkout at Robert Russa Moton High School in Farmville, Virginia, an action led by sixteen-year-old Barbara Rose Johns that produced Davis v. County School Board of Prince Edward County, one of the five cases consolidated into Brown [src: 3, src: 4]. The two 1951 student walkouts, Adkin in Kinston and Moton in Farmville, exemplify what sociologist Aldon Morris characterizes as the pre-existing community organizing infrastructure of Southern Black institutions (churches, schools, NAACP youth councils, fraternal lodges) that made the 1960 student sit-in wave possible [src: 5]. In North Carolina specifically, the NAACP youth-council network and the long pattern of Black-teacher salary-equalization litigation (filed in counties across the state through the 1940s) provided the legal and pedagogical context within which Adkin students would have understood their school's inequality as a litigable grievance rather than a private hardship [src: 6, src: 7]. The historiography of pre-Brown direct action emerging since the 1980s, beginning with Morris's Origins of the Civil Rights Movement, has shifted the periodization of the civil rights movement back from the 1955 Montgomery bus boycott into the 1940s legal and educational organizing that produced both Brown and the cohort of students prepared to mount the post-1960 sit-in and freedom-ride campaigns [src: 5, src: 7].
legacy_and_reception: The Adkin High School walkout has received episodic local commemoration in Lenoir County and broader scholarly attention as the post-2000 historiography of pre-Brown student direct action has expanded. The walkout is documented in the North Carolina African American Heritage Commission's records and in oral histories collected by Eastern North Carolina educational and historical institutions; the Civil Rights History Project's selection of six surviving Adkin walkout veterans for a joint oral history in June 2013 represents the most institutionally durable documentary preservation of the action to date [src: 1, src: 2]. The participants themselves, interviewed in their seventies and eighties, frame the walkout as a self-organized adolescent decision that long predated the adult-led civil rights movement organizations the wider public associates with the era, a framing that aligns with the historiographical correction Aldon Morris's Origins of the Civil Rights Movement and subsequent community-organizing scholarship has pressed [src: 5, src: 8]. The Library of Congress entry for this oral history is one of two CRHP recordings that document pre-Brown K-12 student walkouts (the other being the Barbara Johns family material related to the Farmville Moton walkout), and the joint-interview format the CRHP used here preserves the conversational dynamic among the six participants, including their disagreements about specific dates and adult involvement, that a sequence of single-interviewee recordings would have flattened [src: 2, src: 8]. The 1951 Adkin walkout site itself is not currently listed on the National Register of Historic Places, and the building no longer functions as Adkin High School; recognition of the 1951 action remains primarily an oral-history and community-memory record rather than an architectural or monumental one.

gallery.alt: Judgment of the Supreme Court in Brown v. Board of Education, May 31, 1955, National Archives and Records Administration
gallery.caption: The Supreme Court's implementation decree in Brown v. Board of Education, May 31, 1955, directing federal district judges to oversee desegregation of public schools 'with all deliberate speed.' The Adkin High School students' April 1951 walkout in Kinston, North Carolina occurred three years before the initial Brown ruling (May 1954) and four years before this implementation decree, within months of the Farmville, Virginia Moton High School walkout that became one of the five consolidated Brown cases. The Adkin walkout is documentary evidence that K-12 students were independently mobilizing against the material inequality of segregated schooling on a timeline parallel to the NAACP Legal Defense Fund's litigation strategy, not in response to it. Held by the National Archives and Records Administration (NARA Identifier 301669).
gallery.repository: Wikimedia Commons (sourced from the National Archives and Records Administration, Textual Archives Services Division, Washington, DC)

gallery.alt: Map of state-level public school racial segregation laws in the United States prior to Brown v. Board of Education (1954)
gallery.caption: Map of the legal regime governing public school racial segregation across U.S. states immediately before Brown v. Board of Education. North Carolina, where the Adkin High School walkout took place in April 1951, is in the dark-shaded category of states that legally mandated segregation in public schooling; Adkin was Kinston's all-Black public high school, and the students' walkout was a direct protest of the resource inequality inside the segregated regime depicted on this map. Map by Wikimedia Commons contributor 'King of Hearts,' derivative of a Wikimedia Commons public-domain raster predecessor, distributed under Creative Commons Attribution-ShareAlike 3.0.
gallery.photographer: Wikimedia Commons contributor 'King of Hearts' (SVG vector remake of public-domain raster predecessor)

gallery.alt: The Greensboro Four (David Richmond, Franklin McCain, Ezell A. Blair Jr., and Joseph McNeil) photographed by Jack Moebes for the Greensboro News & Record, February 1960
gallery.caption: The Greensboro Four (David Richmond, Franklin McCain, Ezell A. Blair Jr., and Joseph McNeil) at the Woolworth's lunch counter in Greensboro, North Carolina, February 1960, the action that opened the sit-in wave the cultural reception associates with the start of the modern civil rights student movement. The April 1951 Adkin walkout in Kinston preceded this Greensboro sit-in by nine years and Brown v. Board of Education by three, evidence that the K-12 student-organized direct-action register that the post-1960 sit-in wave is taken to inaugurate had documented precedents in the prewar and immediate postwar period. Photographed by Jack Moebes for the Greensboro News & Record; in the U.S. public domain by reason of pre-1978 publication without copyright notice.
gallery.repository: Wikimedia Commons (sourced from the Greensboro News & Record)
gallery.license: Public domain (United States, pre-1978 publication without copyright notice)

sources.title: John Dudley et al oral history
sources.publisher: Civil Rights History Project, Library of Congress (AFC 2010/039)

sources.title: John Dudley, Eleanor Stewart, Charles Jarmon, Frances Suggs, Harold Suggs, and Samuel Dove oral history
sources.publisher: Civil Rights Digital Library, University System of Georgia

sources.title: Civil Rights History Project, American Folklife Center, About This Collection
sources.publisher: Library of Congress, American Folklife Center (AFC 2010/039 collection description and methodology, including the joint-interview format and the project's commitment to documenting pre-Brown and pre-1960 student direct action)

sources.title: Richard Kluger, Simple Justice: The History of Brown v. Board of Education and Black America's Struggle for Equality
sources.publisher: Alfred A. Knopf, 1976 (revised and expanded edition, Vintage, 2004); the standard institutional history of the NAACP Legal Defense Fund's pre-Brown litigation strategy, the five K-12 cases consolidated as Brown v. Board of Education, and the role of Robert L. Carter (catalog entry 108) and Thurgood Marshall in the equalization-to-direct-challenge pivot of 1949-1950

sources.title: Aldon D. Morris, The Origins of the Civil Rights Movement: Black Communities Organizing for Change
sources.publisher: Free Press, 1984; the foundational sociological history of the pre-1955 organizing infrastructure (Black churches, segregated high schools, NAACP youth councils, college campuses) that made the 1960 sit-in wave possible, and the periodization shift away from Montgomery-as-origin toward 1940s-1950s community organizing as the precondition of the civil rights movement

sources.title: Davison M. Douglas, Reading, Writing, and Race: The Desegregation of the Charlotte Schools
sources.publisher: University of North Carolina Press, 1995; the standard institutional history of North Carolina school desegregation litigation through the 1971 Swann v. Charlotte-Mecklenburg busing decision, documenting the equalization-era NAACP teacher-salary cases filed across North Carolina counties through the 1940s and the legal and pedagogical context in which the 1951 Adkin walkout's grievance would have been understood by students and parents

sources.title: David S. Cecelski, Along Freedom Road: Hyde County, North Carolina, and the Fate of Black Schools in the South
sources.publisher: University of North Carolina Press, 1994; the standard institutional history of an eastern North Carolina Black community's response to school desegregation, including the loss of all-Black schools as institutions of community-formed identity and the broader regional context of African American education in the eastern coastal-plain counties (Lenoir County, where Adkin sits, is in the same physiographic and educational-history region treated in this study)

sources.title: Charles M. Payne and Adam Green, eds., Time Longer than Rope: A Century of African American Activism, 1850-1950
sources.publisher: New York University Press, 2003; documentary anthology and scholarly essays on the long-arc tradition of African American activism preceding the post-1955 civil rights movement, including pre-Brown student direct action and equalization-era community organizing across the South
